perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby agree to retain, and to require all respective representatives to reta in, the Inform ation provided pursuant to this Agreem ent in strict confidence in accordance with the term s set forth below.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties will endeavor to retain the Information, and will encourage all respective representatives to retain the Information provided pursuant to this Agreement in reasonable confidence.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'hereby agree to retain in strict confidence' with 'will endeavor to retain... in reasonable confidence' weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor' and 'reasonable' introduce ambiguity, making the requirement less stringent and enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325E.64 subd. 1 defines a private business's duty to protect data, including requiring implementation of reasonable security procedures. The modification replaces 'strict confidence' with 'reasonable confidence', implying a lower standard than what the law expects of private data, potentially leading to insufficient data protection measures. This introduces the ambiguity of whether the 'reasonable confidence' standard is enough to satisfy the legal requirements of "reasonable security procedures".
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325E.64 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325E Section 325E.64 325E.63 325E.65 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325E Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325E.64 Version List Recent History 2007 325E.64 New 2007 c 108 s 1 325E.64 ACCESS DEVICES; BREACH OF SECURITY. § Subdivision 1. Definitions. (a) For purposes of this section, the terms defined in this subdivision have the meanings given them. (b) "Access device" means a card issued by a financial institution that contains a magnetic stripe, microprocessor chip, or other means for storage of information which includes, but is not limited to, a credit card, debit card, or stored value card. (c) "Breach of the security of the system" has the meaning given in section 325E.61 , subdivision 1, paragraph (d). (d) "Card security code" means the three-digit or four-digit value printed on an access device or contained in the microprocessor chip or magnetic stripe of an access device which is used to validate access device information during the authorization process. (e) "Financial institution" means any office of a bank, bank and trust, trust company with banking powers, savings bank, industrial loan company, savings association, credit union, or regulated lender. (f) "Microprocessor chip data" means the data contained in the microprocessor chip of an access device. (g) "Magnetic stripe data" means the data contained in the magnetic stripe of an access device. (h) "PIN" means a personal identification code that identifies the cardholder. (i) "PIN verification code number" means the data used to verify cardholder
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'strict confidence' while the changed text requires 'reasonable confidence.' The perturbation explanation states that 'endeavor' and 'reasonable' introduce ambiguity, making the requirement less stringent and enforceable. The Law Explanation states that replacing 'strict confidence' with 'reasonable confidence', implies a lower standard than what the law expects of private data. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines breach of security and mentions reasonable security procedures. This supports that weakening the language to 'reasonable confidence' could be insufficient and contradict the legal expectation of 'reasonable security procedures,' thus creating a contradiction under the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: In further consideration of Minnesota Power disclosing its Information hereunder, Customer agrees that it will not use the Informa tion for any third-party’s benefit or in providing services to third parties (which shall specifically include any wholesale or retail customer served by Minnesota Power).
perturbation.changed_text: In further consideration of Minnesota Power disclosing its Information hereunder, Customer agrees to generally refrain from using the Information for any third-party’s direct benefit or in knowingly providing similar services to third parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not use' to 'generally refrain from using' and adding 'direct benefit' and 'knowingly providing similar services' creates ambiguity. What constitutes 'generally refrain,' 'direct benefit,' and 'knowingly providing similar services' are open to interpretation, weakening the original prohibition.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325C.01 defines and protects trade secrets. The modification potentially undermines this protection. By introducing 'generally refrain' and 'direct benefit,' the agreement creates loopholes where the customer could argue that certain uses of the information, even if beneficial to a third party, don't violate the agreement because they were not 'direct' or the customer didn't 'knowingly' provide services. This allows for the misappropriation of trade secrets under the guise of 'general' refraining, thereby contradicting the strict protections intended by the Minnesota Uniform Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325C.01 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325C Section 325C.01 325C.02 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325C Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325C.01 Version List 325C.01 DEFINITIONS. § Subdivision 1. Scope. As used in sections 325C.01 to 325C.07 , the terms defined in these sections have the meanings given them, unless the context requires otherwise. § Subd. 2. Improper means. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. § Subd. 3. Misappropriation. "Misappropriation" means: (i) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the discloser's or user's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of the discloser's or user's position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. § Subd. 4. Person.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces ambiguity, weakening the protection of Minnesota Power's information. The original text's strict prohibition ('will not use') is softened to 'generally refrain from using,' and the addition of 'direct benefit' and 'knowingly providing similar services' creates loopholes. Minnesota Statutes § 325C.01 defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By allowing for interpretations where the customer could argue they are 'generally refraining' or not providing 'direct benefit,' the agreement potentially contradicts the statute's intent to prevent unauthorized use of trade secrets. The change could allow for the 'misappropriation' of information if the customer claims they didn't 'knowingly' provide similar services, thereby weakening the legal protection intended by the statute.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be effective for a term of ______ (__) years from the day first written above. Notwithstanding the Parties entering into any further agreement or arrangement, or any other event or occurrence, the provisions of this Agreement relating to nondisclosure (including the provisions relating to the breach of this Agreement) or use of the Information shall remain in full force and eff ect for ________ years or as long as the Information is confidential and non-public, whichever is less, unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be effective for a term of ______ (__) years from the day first written above. Notwithstanding the Parties entering into any further agreement or arrangement, or any other event or occurrence, the provisions of this Agreement relating to nondisclosure will ideally remain in effect for a period that the Parties find reasonable.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the nondisclosure agreement to 'ideally remain in effect for a period that the Parties find reasonable' introduces considerable ambiguity regarding the duration of the nondisclosure obligation. It replaces a defined timeframe with a subjective assessment of reasonableness, making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325E.68 discusses the protection of trade secrets and requires reasonable measures to protect them. By making the duration of the nondisclosure agreement indefinite and subject to the parties' subjective sense of 'reasonableness,' the modified agreement weakens the protection of trade secrets. A period determined to be 'reasonable' by the parties might be insufficient to protect the confidentiality of the information, thereby violating the statute's implied requirement of reasonable efforts and measures to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a definite timeframe for nondisclosure with a subjective assessment of "reasonableness." The Law Explanation suggests this undermines the protection of trade secrets under Minnesota Statutes § 325E.68, which implies 'reasonable efforts and measures' to maintain secrecy. By making the duration indefinite and subject to the parties' subjective view, the modified agreement weakens the protection, because what the parties find "reasonable" may be insufficient to maintain confidentiality.
